text.title: Feature Flag Types
text.summary: Five flag categories with lifespan expectations and intended use cases.

rule.statement: Classify every flag as one of: release (hide incomplete code), experiment (A/B test), ops (operational control), permission (access gating), or kill-switch (emergency disable).
rule.rationale: Category determines expected lifespan and cleanup policy. Mixing types leads to flags that live indefinitely without a plan for removal.

list.item: Release — lifespan days to weeks; removed after full rollout; example: new checkout flow.
Experiment — lifespan weeks to months; removed after statistical significance reached; example: pricing page A/B test.
Ops — lifespan medium to long; controls circuit breakers, maintenance mode; example: disable background jobs during incident.
Permission — lifespan permanent (by design); gates premium or beta features per user/group; example: beta access tier.
Kill-switch — lifespan permanent; always available for emergency disable; example: disable payment processing.

rule.statement: Every release and experiment flag must have an owner and an expected_cleanup date recorded in the flag registry at creation time.
rule.rationale: Flags without ownership become orphaned. Without a cleanup date, they accumulate indefinitely — each adds a branch to every affected code path.

rule.statement: Run a stale-flag audit in CI: fail the build (or emit a warning) when a flag's expected_cleanup date has passed.
rule.rationale: Automated enforcement is the only reliable way to prevent flag debt. Manual audits are consistently skipped under delivery pressure.
